---
title: "Chapter 7: Drill planning under uncertainty"
subtitle: "Sequential drill planning on the BC Golden Triangle regional starting map"
sidebar: drill_planning
---

[← back to portfolio map](index.html)

## Setup

The earlier chapters in this portfolio build prospectivity maps. A
prospectivity map is a per-cell probability surface, in this case
"per-250 m grid cell, the model's estimate of how likely an unmined
porphyry-copper deposit is to be there." That map by itself does not
tell you where to put the next drill hole. Real exploration campaigns
are sequential. Each hole costs money, returns information, and that
information should update the next-hole decision.

This chapter ports the methodology of *Intelligent Prospector* (Mern
et al., *Geosci. Model Dev.* 16:289, 2023; arXiv 2410.10610, 2024)
onto the BC Golden Triangle regional starting map from Chapter 2.
Both versions of that paper model drilling as a **POMDP**, which is
worth a sentence of explanation before we go further.

A **POMDP** ("partially observable Markov decision process") is the
math framework for making sequential decisions when you cannot see
the state of the world directly but can pay to gather noisy hints
about it. Drilling fits the shape naturally: the state of the world
is the unknown layout of ore underground, the noisy hints are the
drill assays you get back from each hole, and the decisions are
where to drill next. Each decision changes what you can learn for
the next decision, so a good planner has to weigh the immediate
payoff against the value of the information it would gain.

I'll keep the math light. Where a technical term first appears I'll
explain it in plain language; the same terms are collected at the
bottom of the chapter in [Terminology](#terminology) for quick
reference.

## The result on synthetic terrain

The first question to settle: does the planning machinery actually
beat the simplest baseline (drill the cell with the highest
probability under the starting map) when the conditions are
favorable to planning? The 2024 paper's headline test runs a 30 by
30 grid with two features that make planning meaningful:
neighbor-correlation in the prior (drilling cell A tells you
something about its neighbors) and noisy sensor readings. Their
ported version, run here on the BC Golden Triangle starting map:

![Four policies on a synthetic 30x30 stress test matching the 2024 Mern paper's conditions (correlated prior, noisy Gaussian sensor, 9 holes per episode, 17 episodes). Left: fraction of episodes that have found at least one true deposit at each drill step. POMCP finishes at 94%, BayesianGreedy at 82%, GreedyMean (the simplest baseline) saturates at 53% by hole four. Right: mean discovery rate per trajectory.](./data/derived/bcgt/fig_v20_b1_baseline_benchmark.png)

The four policies on the chart are different ways of choosing the
next hole:

- **Random** picks any un-drilled cell at random, ignoring everything
  the prior says. The control case.
- **GreedyMean** drills the cell with the single highest probability
  from the starting map, every step. Never updates. It is a static
  recommendation list, not a planner.
- **BayesianGreedy** drills the highest-probability cell, but after
  every observation it updates the probability surface using Bayes'
  rule. So each step's pick reflects everything seen so far. It
  thinks one step ahead.
- **POMCP** ("Partially Observable Monte Carlo Planning", Silver and
  Veness 2010) does the same update, but before choosing the next
  hole it simulates many possible futures (its own next several
  drills plus the imagined observations) and picks the action whose
  simulated futures look best on average. Think of it as: "imagine
  the next five rolls of the dice many times, see which opening
  move tends to come out ahead, do that move."

POMCP finds at least one deposit in 94% of episodes by the last hole.
GreedyMean stalls at 53% by hole four and never improves: it has no
mechanism to learn from what it has already drilled. BayesianGreedy
reaches 82%, getting most of the way to POMCP by belief-updating
alone.

Two effects are stacked here. The first is **belief updating with
spatial correlation**. The prior carries the assumption that a drill
at cell A tells you something about cells B, C, D within roughly a
kilometer (this is the "GP-correlated" prior, where GP stands for
**Gaussian process**: a probability distribution over a smoothly-
varying surface, calibrated so neighboring cells covary the way
sound-rock geology actually does). GreedyMean ignores that and leaves
information on the table after every hole. BayesianGreedy uses it.
The second effect is **multi-step lookahead**: POMCP weighs not just
"what is the next hole worth in immediate reward" but "what does the
next hole's observation buy me for the holes after that." The first
effect is the big jump (53% to 82%). The second effect is the
smaller tail win (82% to 94%).

A small aside: an older 2023 version of the paper used independent
Bernoulli cells (no spatial correlation between neighbors) and a
noiseless sensor. Under those conditions one-step greedy is provably
optimal, and POMCP ties greedy on this same starting map. The 2024
paper added the two ingredients that make planning win.

## Real-data check: BCGT retrospective across 7 mining districts

The chart above is a stress test on synthetic terrain. The real-data
check is what an exploration geologist would actually do: given the
historic BCGS drilling record up through 2009, which cells would each
policy recommend, and how many of those recommendations would have
landed on cells where post-2010 operators eventually found ore (Cu
assays above the 0.2% Cox-Singer porphyry-Cu cutoff)?

I identified the seven BCGS mining districts where post-2010 Cu+
drilling actually happened by running DBSCAN on the 47 post-2010 Cu+
cells in the 500 m BCGT grid. Four matched named public districts by
lat/lon proximity (KSM, Brucejack, Red Chris, Snip); three are
unnamed exploration corridors between named districts. For each, I
took a 50 by 50 cell working area (25 km by 25 km) centered on the
cluster centroid, excluded the pre-2010 drilled cells from the
candidate set, and scored each policy on **capture@N**: of the N
cells it recommends first, what fraction would have hit a post-2010
Cu+ ground-truth cell?

The two informative findings come from the **pre-2010 leak-free
prior**: a prior built only from pre-2010 BCGS Cu+ assays in the
subarea, Gaussian-smoothed at 1.5 km. By construction this map only
knows what was known before 2010. It cannot have been informed by
post-2010 discoveries.

![Capture@N rate across the 7 BCGT mining districts under the pre-2010 leak-free prior. X-axis: N (the drill budget on log scale, from 10 to 625 cells out of the 2500-cell working area). Y-axis: fraction of post-2010 Cu+ ground-truth cells captured in the policy's first N picks. One line per policy. Lead findings: at Red_Chris, BayesianGreedy captures 54% of post-2010 Cu+ cells in 625 picks vs Static prior's 31%. At KSM, POMCP captures 50% vs Static prior's 43%. Most other districts have too few positives (2-5 cells) for the curves to differentiate above noise.](./data/derived/bcgt/fig_v20_b2_multi_subarea_leakfree.png)

The reading.

**Red Chris (13 post-2010 Cu+ cells in subarea):** BayesianGreedy
captures 54% in 625 picks against Static prior's 31% (23 percentage
points). This is the cleanest case for the Bayesian-updating policy
beating the static recommendation on real-data BCGT terrain. POMCP
sits in the middle at 38%. Random captures 15%.

**KSM (14 post-2010 Cu+ cells):** POMCP captures 50% against Static
prior's 43% (7 percentage points), with BayesianGreedy lagging both
at 29%. The KSM cluster contains the working area the original B.2
benchmark was run on; expanding to capture@N at smaller drill budgets
exposes the rank order more cleanly than the original capture@k%
budget.

**Brucejack (3 positives in subarea, small sample):** BayesianGreedy
is the first policy to find any post-2010 Cu+ at all (33% at N=100).
Static prior catches up at N=625 but BayesianGreedy was there first.

The other four districts have 2 to 5 post-2010 Cu+ cells in their
subareas (mostly outside the current named-district inventory), so
the per-curve variance is wide enough that "POMCP wins" or "Static
wins" reads as a coin flip. The fair reading: this benchmark works
for districts with at least about 10 positive cells; the others
establish the search procedure but not differential policy quality.

### A small caveat on the informative MINFILE prior

The chapter's original 3-panel KSM chart used a smoothed MINFILE
mineral-occurrence prior alongside the leak-free pre-2010 prior. On
that informative-but-temporally-contaminated prior, Static prior
captures 86% of the post-2010 Cu+ cells at KSM in the top 25%, with
POMCP at 79% and BayesianGreedy at 43%.

![Three starting-map variants on the KSM subarea (legacy single-district B.2 chart). Left: smoothed MINFILE mineral-occurrence prior (informative but temporally contaminated since MINFILE is a current snapshot, so it includes occurrence entries created after 2010). Center: pre-2010 leak-free prior. Right: uniform prior (planner starts blind).](./data/derived/bcgt/fig_v20_b2_retrospective_capture.png)

That MINFILE-informative win for Static prior is partly an artifact:
MINFILE is a current snapshot of known mineral occurrences, and many
of the BCGS drillholes that hit the Cox-Singer cutoff after 2010 are
now MINFILE entries themselves. The prior knows the answer. So the
informative-prior chart is the right comparison for "Static prior
is hard to beat when the prior is excellent" but not for "is planning
useful in real exploration where the planner only has access to
historic data?"

The leak-free chart at the top of this section is the cleaner test of
the latter question, and there the Bayesian-updating policies start
to earn their value on the districts with enough positive cells to
measure.

## How the policies hold up under noisy binary sensors

The synthetic chart at the top uses a Gaussian-continuous sensor
(a drill returns a grade reading plus noise). Real drill assays come
back as quantified values, but in a hand-mapping or first-look
reconnaissance the operator decision is often binary: above or below
the cutoff. The 2024 paper models that case with a **Bernoulli
sensor** with two sensitivity parameters: $\alpha$ (the false-positive
rate, how often the sensor says "yes" when there is no deposit) and
$\beta$ (the false-negative rate, how often it says "no" when there
is a deposit). The Bernoulli distribution itself is just the math
name for a single coin flip with a tunable bias: the bias is the
probability of "heads." For our drill, "heads" is "sensor says ore is
here." The bias depends on whether there actually IS ore there: the
bias is $1-\beta$ if there is, $\alpha$ if there isn't. Realistic
values for porphyry-Cu reconnaissance drilling are $\alpha = 0.05$
to $0.10$ and $\beta = 0.10$ to $0.20$, based on Mern et al.'s
calibration against the RC drilling literature.

C.1 sweeps a 3-by-3 grid of $(\alpha, \beta)$ values to see how each
policy holds up as sensor quality varies.

![Mean discovery rate per policy across 9 (alpha, beta) combinations on the same 30x30 synthetic subarea as the B.1 chart. Random sits at 0.06 across the whole plane and GreedyMean sits at 0.32: neither uses observations, so they don't respond to sensor quality. BayesianGreedy ranges 0.48-0.54 and POMCP ranges 0.43-0.57 depending on the sensor parameters; both policies degrade modestly as the sensor gets noisier, and POMCP holds a small edge over BayesianGreedy at most cells. The within-row variation includes 17-episode Monte Carlo noise; the relative ordering is the chapter point, not the precise cell values.](./data/derived/bcgt/fig_v20_c1_bernoulli_sweep.png)

The reading. Even with substantial sensor noise (10% false positive
plus 20% false negative, the worst cell), the machinery extracts most
of the value from observations: BayesianGreedy and POMCP land roughly
18 to 25 points above the static-prior baseline (0.32). The
sensitivity to sensor quality is modest: dropping the false-positive
rate from 10% to 2% buys at most a few discovery-rate points. The
single-hypothesis B.1 finding holds under the Bernoulli sensor: most
of the win is correlation-aware belief updating; multi-step lookahead
is a smaller increment on top.

## When the starting map itself is wrong

The B.1, B.2, and C.1 charts all assume the planner's starting map is
a fair approximation of the truth, even when noisy. C.2 handles the
harder case: what if NEITHER of the competing geological stories the
planner considers is right?

This is where the chapter starts using **hypotheses** the way the
POMDP literature does: a hypothesis is a complete geological story
about the area (e.g., "the deposit is the upper plume of a porphyry
system localized at fault X" vs. "the deposit is a skarn body
attached to intrusion Y"). Each hypothesis comes with its own
expected probability surface over cells. A **single-hypothesis**
planner carries one such story. A **multi-hypothesis** planner
carries several at once and tracks how confident it is in each one
("the belief over hypotheses") as observations come in.

Mern et al. 2024 add a **null hypothesis** to the set: a
maximum-entropy mixture with no spatial structure, which corresponds
to "actually, nothing here resembles any of our geological stories."
After every drill the planner updates a categorical posterior over
the whole hypothesis set including the null. When the null becomes
the most-supported hypothesis, the planner flags "the geological
model doesn't explain what we're seeing" rather than continuing to
fit a wrong story. This is the falsification check.

![Categorical posterior over a 3-hypothesis set across 25 random drills under three ground-truth scenarios. Left: ground truth drawn from hypothesis A; the posterior locks onto h_A by drill 3. Center: ground truth from hypothesis B; h_B wins by drill 5-7. Right: ground truth from the null (no spatial structure); the falsification flag fires at drill 2 because both paper hypotheses immediately fail to explain observations the null predicts.](./data/derived/bcgt/fig_v20_c2_falsification_demo.png)

The reading. The falsification check fires fast when neither paper
hypothesis matches the data. The two paper-hypothesis scenarios also
converge cleanly to the correct hypothesis within 5 to 7 drills,
which demonstrates that the categorical update mechanism doesn't
degrade the planner's identification ability.

## SARSOP and alpha vectors

The multi-hypothesis machinery raises a planning question: at each
step, how does the planner USE the current belief over hypotheses to
pick the next drill? POMCP can do it (the simulated futures sample
across hypotheses every rollout), but POMCP runs a fresh tree search
at every step, which can get expensive when the hypothesis set is
large.

**SARSOP** is a different solver, attractive when the problem is
small enough to enumerate. SARSOP stands for **Successive
Approximations of the Reachable Space under Optimal Policies**, from
Kurniawati, Hsu, and Lee (2008). It solves the same POMDP that POMCP
solves, but with a different strategy: it pre-computes a policy once,
ahead of time, and at decision time it looks up the answer in
microseconds.

POMCP is a "live" planner. SARSOP is a "pre-computed" planner. The
POMDP literature calls these "online" and "offline" planning
respectively, but those words mean something very different in
everyday computing (internet vs. not), so I'll stick with "live" and
"pre-computed" through the rest of the chapter.

### What is an alpha vector

The thing SARSOP pre-computes is a collection of objects called
**alpha vectors**. An alpha vector is just a list of numbers, one per
hypothesis. Each number says: "if hypothesis $i$ were the truth, what
would the value of taking this action and then playing optimally
afterward be." There are typically many alpha vectors, each one tied
to a particular action plan.

A tiny worked example makes this concrete. Suppose you are choosing
between two drill sites and you have two competing geological
stories. Theory A says the ore is in the north. Theory B says the ore
is in the south. Drilling at the ore location pays +10 (the
discovery). Drilling at the wrong location costs 2 (a wasted hole).

The reward table:

|  | Truth = A | Truth = B |
|--|--:|--:|
| Drill north | +10 | -2 |
| Drill south | -2 | +10 |

Each action gets an alpha vector. In this one-shot case the alpha
vector is just the row of the reward table:

- Alpha vector for "drill north": $[+10, -2]$
- Alpha vector for "drill south": $[-2, +10]$

Read those as: "if I drill north, my reward will be +10 in a world
where Theory A is the truth, or -2 in a world where Theory B is the
truth."

### What is a belief, and how does the dot product work

The **belief** is just a list of probabilities, one per hypothesis,
that sum to 1. It says how confident you are in each story right now.

- $[0.5, 0.5]$: no idea which theory is right. **Uniform belief.**
- $[0.7, 0.3]$: leaning toward A.
- $[0.05, 0.95]$: strongly thinking B.
- $[1.0, 0.0]$: certain it's A.

Belief is built by Bayes' rule. You start with a **starting belief**
(the prior, which here is $[0.5, 0.5]$ since you have no reason to
favor either story before any drilling). After each drill, you
multiply each component of the belief by how likely the reading you
got would be under each hypothesis, then renormalize so the
components still sum to 1. Each observation slides the belief
somewhere.

To pick the next action, take the **dot product** of your current
belief and each action's alpha vector. The dot product is the
expected reward of taking that action under your current belief: each
component of the belief weights the corresponding component of the
alpha vector.

At belief $[0.5, 0.5]$:

- Drill north: $0.5 \times 10 + 0.5 \times (-2) = +4$
- Drill south: $0.5 \times (-2) + 0.5 \times 10 = +4$
- Tie. Either action is fine when you have no information.

At belief $[0.7, 0.3]$ (you lean toward A):

- Drill north: $0.7 \times 10 + 0.3 \times (-2) = +6.4$
- Drill south: $0.7 \times (-2) + 0.3 \times 10 = +1.6$
- Drill north wins. Makes sense: you believe A more, so the action
  whose payoff is high under A is best.

At belief $[0.05, 0.95]$ (you strongly think B):

- Drill north: $0.05 \times 10 + 0.95 \times (-2) = -1.4$
- Drill south: $0.05 \times (-2) + 0.95 \times 10 = +9.4$
- Drill south wins big.

The maximum dot product across all alpha vectors at a given belief is
the expected reward of the best action at that belief. The alpha
vector that achieves the maximum tells you which action to take. So
the policy at decision time is: take the dot product of your current
belief against every alpha vector you have, pick the largest, do its
action. Microseconds.

### Where do the alpha vectors come from in the multi-step case

In the one-shot example above the alpha vector is just the immediate
reward table. Easy. In real POMDPs you have a sequence of decisions,
and each step's choice affects what you can learn for the next step.
There, an alpha vector encodes "if the truth is hypothesis $i$, and I
take this action now and **then play optimally afterward**, the
discounted sum of all my future rewards is this number." That harder
computation is what SARSOP does.

There is usually more than one alpha vector per action in the
multi-step case, because the optimal "what to do afterward" depends
on what observation you get next. So SARSOP can output dozens or
hundreds of alpha vectors total.

SARSOP builds them by **successive approximation** (the SA in the
name). Roughly:

1. Start at the last step. The future is empty, so the alpha vectors
   are just the immediate reward table.
2. Work backward one step. For each action, combine its immediate
   reward with the expected best alpha vector at the next step
   (averaged over all observations you might receive). That gives
   you new alpha vectors for the current step.
3. Prune. Many of these new vectors are never the best at any
   belief. Throw those out.
4. Iterate. Keep going backward and pruning until the value function
   stops changing meaningfully.

The surviving alpha vectors collectively define the **value
function** over the entire belief space: at any belief, the highest
dot product tells you both the best achievable future value and the
action that achieves it.

The geometric picture: think of each alpha vector as a flat tilted
plane over the space of possible beliefs. The maximum of all those
planes is a piecewise-linear surface, which is the value function.
SARSOP precomputes that surface once. The policy is a fast lookup
at decision time.

### Does SARSOP help in practice

The 2024 paper argues SARSOP matters at multi-hypothesis scale because
POMCP's value-function variance grows with the hypothesis count,
while SARSOP's pre-computation is amortized. To check that, I needed
a problem where the planning advantage is actually present. My first
attempt was a 5-by-5 grid with 4 corner-deposit hypotheses plus a
null. SARSOP, POMCP, and greedy-MAP all tied at 74% discovery on
that one. The reason: each hypothesis placed its deposit at a unique
cell with no penalty for guessing wrong, so the one-step-greedy
policy "drill the highest-expected-deposit cell" was already optimal.
Multi-step lookahead had nothing to add.

To actually expose the planning advantage I rebuilt the experiment as
a **Tiger-style** problem. The original Tiger problem (Kaelbling,
Littman, and Cassandra 1998) is the textbook POMDP where you choose
between opening one of two doors (committing to a guess, with a big
reward if right and a big penalty if wrong) and listening (gathering
information at a small cost). The optimal play is usually to listen
a few times to build confidence before committing. My drilling
analog of Tiger has four cells:

Two **"claim"** cells, one for each competing geological story. A
claim cell stands in for a drill target each story would recommend:
Story A says "the ore is here," and claim A is the cell you would
drill under that story. Drilling a claim pays +50 if the matching
story is the truth and -30 if the other one is. The -30 represents
what happens in real exploration when you burn a $1M hole on the
wrong target while the actual deposit sits undrilled. Claim cells do
not give you informative sensor readings: the sensor at a claim
cell fires the same way regardless of which story is true.

Two **"marker"** cells, one per story. A marker stands in for a
piece of discriminating evidence the two stories predict differently:
a magnetic anomaly, a geochemical halo, a structural feature, a
logging response. Marker A reads positive when Story A is true and
stays quiet when Story B is true; Marker B is the opposite. Drilling
a marker costs the same drill cost (-1) but pays no discovery reward
and no penalty. Markers are pure information.

A uniform starting belief over A and B, a 5-drill budget, and the
same Bernoulli sensor noise as the earlier experiments. This is the
case where the wiring earns its keep: greedy under uniform belief
picks one of the claim cells immediately and pays the
wrong-commitment penalty on half the episodes, while a smart planner
spends one drill on the appropriate marker and commits only after
the belief concentrates.

![Three-panel comparison on the Tiger-style problem. Left: mean discounted reward with one-standard-error bars. Center: how often each policy drilled the wrong claim cell. Right: discovery rate (right claim eventually drilled within the budget). 200 episodes, gamma=0.95, sensor (alpha, beta) = (0.10, 0.10), wrong-commitment penalty 30.](./data/derived/bcgt/fig_v20_sarsop_marker_advantage.png)

The reading. Greedy-MAP averages +1 reward, drills the wrong claim
on 56% of episodes, and finds the right claim on only 45% of them.
It commits early and gets stuck drilling the wrong cell for the rest
of the budget. POMCP and SARSOP both use the markers to
disambiguate first. POMCP averages +31 with a 28% wrong-commit rate;
SARSOP averages +37 with a 2% wrong-commit rate. SARSOP comes out
ahead of POMCP here because the alpha-vector policy is computed
against the full belief space rather than an MCTS sample of it; on a
small problem with a high penalty for committing under uncertainty,
that extra precision matters. The gap closes on harder problems
where the pre-computation becomes the bottleneck.

The widget below plays one episode at a time so the mechanism is
visible turn-by-turn. Each panel shows the same 4 cells (two claims,
two markers), each policy's belief over Hypothesis A vs B as a
colored bar, the cell currently being drilled, and the running
reward. Press Play, then step backward or forward by drill. Greedy
locks onto a claim cell and stays there because drilling a claim
returns no information about which story is true. SARSOP picks the
appropriate marker first, the belief swings, then it commits. You
will sometimes see SARSOP drill the same marker twice before
committing: a single positive reading at a marker still leaves a 10%
chance the sensor false-positived, and a second positive reading
cuts that to about 1%. The solver decides the extra drill is worth
it because it reduces the cost of a wrong commit on the next step.
You will also see policies re-drill cells after they have already
found the deposit: the toy problem fixes the budget at 5 drills
without a "stop" action, so re-drilling a known-good cell is the
least-bad use of leftover budget.

{{< include _widget_v20_marker_animation.qmd >}}

## Scaling up to the BCGT grid

The Tiger problem proved the SARSOP machinery works on a 4-cell toy
problem where the planning advantage is engineered to be present.
The next question is whether it scales onto the actual BCGT subarea:
a 30 by 30 grid (15 km by 15 km at 500 m per cell), with the same
GP kernel parameters the rest of the chapter uses (Matern smoothness
parameter $\nu=2.5$, marginal standard deviation $\sigma=0.1$,
correlation length $\ell=1500$ m). Directly handing SARSOP a 900-cell
action space would not terminate in any reasonable time. The
alpha-vector count grows fast with state and action dimensionality.
900 actions are too many.

The fix is **top-K candidate pruning**. At each drill step the
planner computes the expected deposit probability per cell under its
current belief over hypotheses, picks the top K = 20 un-drilled
cells by that expectation, compresses those cells into a small POMDP
(K actions, $N$ hypothesis states, binary observations), and solves
it with SARSOP. The candidate set changes every step as the belief
updates; the pre-computation runs fresh on each step's subproblem.
On the 30-by-30 setup with 3 hypotheses, that subproblem solves in
0.02 seconds. A full 9-drill episode costs under a second of
planning time.

To probe the scale-up I built two synthetic GP-correlated hypotheses
on the BCGT grid plus the null. H_NW places a Gaussian deposit
anomaly in the northwest quadrant; H_SE places one in the southeast
quadrant. Both share the same kernel parameters; only the prior
mean fields differ.

![D.1.A: the three starting maps on the BCGT subarea, plus the expected deposit probability under a uniform belief over the three hypotheses. Red markers show the top 20 candidate cells SARSOP would consider first under the uniform belief: they split between the two blob centers, because the planner has no reason yet to favor either hypothesis.](./data/derived/bcgt/fig_v20_d1_priors_setup.png)

The benchmark runs 60 episodes. For each episode, a fresh GP
realization is drawn under whichever hypothesis is the chosen truth,
and the deposit cells are the cells where that realization exceeds
the cutoff. This is the per-episode-resampling fix; an earlier
version of this benchmark reused one canonical realization across
all episodes, which artificially reduced variance and gave the
planners more knowledge of the truth than they would have in real
exploration. The truth distribution is uniform across H_NW, H_SE,
and H_null. A 9-drill budget, $\gamma = 0.95$, and the same Bernoulli
sensor noise as the earlier experiments. The reward is **one-shot
discovery**: each episode pays the +50 discovery bonus once, on the
first deposit cell drilled. Subsequent drills only pay the -1 drill
cost. This matches the Tiger experiment's "did you find it" semantic
and the real-exploration intuition that finding the deposit is the
milestone, not the count of deposit-region cells drilled. Both
planners (POMCP and SARSOP) operate on the same top-K=20 candidate
set per step, with POMCP running 10,000 simulations per step.

![D.1.C: BCGT-scale benchmark. Left: overall cumulative reward across the 60 episodes, one-standard-error bars. Center: reward broken down by ground truth (H_NW, H_SE, H_null). Right: fraction of episodes where the policy found the deposit within the 9-drill budget; the dashed line is the ceiling (the fraction of episodes that have a deposit to find at all).](./data/derived/bcgt/fig_v20_d1_benchmark.png)

The reading, and an honest correction from an earlier draft of this
chapter. All four policies tie within one standard error of the
mean: random +17.5, greedy +16.7, POMCP +16.6, SARSOP +16.1. The
SARSOP-wins-by-57-points narrative an earlier version of this page
reported was an artifact of two methodology shortcuts I have since
fixed. First, that version reused one canonical GP realization for
the truth across all episodes, which gave the planners exact
knowledge of where deposits would be under each hypothesis. Second,
it paid +50 for every new deposit cell drilled, so policies that
correctly identified the right region collected many +50 rewards per
episode. Per-episode resampling and one-shot discovery together
remove both artifacts. With those fixes, on this setup, planning and
greedy come out the same.

SARSOP does still win on **belief-tracking accuracy**: the final
posterior probability it assigns to the true hypothesis averages 0.82
across episodes, against POMCP's 0.77, greedy's 0.67, and random's
0.51. SARSOP knows which story is true with high confidence by the
end of the budget. But the immediate-reward metric doesn't reward
that knowledge, because the budget is over by then and finding a
single deposit cell is the only payoff. SARSOP's superior posterior
would translate to better decisions in a longer campaign where the
identified hypothesis informs follow-up drilling.

Two sensitivity checks against the methodology choices:

![D.1.C K-sweep. Mean discounted reward for the four policies as the top-K candidate set is varied across {5, 10, 20, 50}. 30 episodes per condition, POMCP at 1000 simulations per step, per-episode truth realization, one-shot discovery reward.](./data/derived/bcgt/fig_v20_d1_k_sweep.png)

![D.1.C POMCP-sims sweep. Mean discounted reward for POMCP across {200, 1000, 5000, 10000} simulations per step, at K=20. Random, greedy, and SARSOP shown as constant references (they don't depend on POMCP's simulation count).](./data/derived/bcgt/fig_v20_d1_pomcp_sims_sweep.png)

The reading on both. The K choice barely moves the needle, which
says the candidate pool is informative enough at small K. The POMCP
simulation count plateaus by about 1000 sims and does not catch
SARSOP at any tested value, but the gap is within SEM throughout
(see [Findings](#findings) for the full caveat list).

### Why doesn't SARSOP win here when it won in the Tiger experiment?

The Tiger problem had two specific ingredients that the BCGT
setup does not. First, **cost asymmetry between exploration and
commitment**: claim cells could pay you nothing or lose you 30 points;
marker cells only ever cost the drill cost. So information was cheap
relative to commitment risk. Second, **claim cells had zero
information value**: drilling them taught you nothing about which
story was true, only whether you got lucky. In the BCGT setup,
drilling ANY cell gives you Bernoulli evidence about which hypothesis
matches the data, and there is no extra penalty for drilling the
wrong region beyond just not collecting the +50 discovery. So the
greedy strategy (drill the highest-expected cell, get either a
discovery or a Bernoulli signal that updates your belief) is hard
to beat. The planning advantage that showed up in Tiger is not
universal; it depends on the structure of the reward and observation
model.

A future extension would re-introduce the Tiger structure at scale:
each hypothesis has a small "claim region" (drilling cells outside
the truth's claim region under truth=h gives a penalty), plus
explicit marker cells. Whether that structure exists in real BCGT
exploration is a domain question I will take on in D.1.D, where the
hypothesis set is replaced with real BCGS deposit-type splits from
the dh2loop labels covered in Chapter 2.

## Findings

A consolidated list of caveats from this chapter, by milestone.

**B.1 (synthetic).** The 30-by-30 stress test uses GP-correlated
priors with one true geological story per episode. POMCP wins at 94%
discovery vs greedy's 53%. This is the planning advantage as
described in the paper. The win is largely driven by belief updating
with spatial correlation; multi-step lookahead is a smaller tail
gain.

**B.2 (real-data retrospective, multi-subarea).** The check now spans
7 BCGT mining districts identified by DBSCAN on the BCGS post-2010
Cu+ point cloud (KSM, Brucejack, Red Chris, Snip, plus three unnamed
exploration corridors). Different deposit types (epithermal Au, skarn,
VMS) within those districts are not separated; future work could
re-run per deposit type. The strongest positive findings come from
the pre-2010 leak-free prior (Red Chris BayesianGreedy +23 pp over
Static, KSM POMCP +7 pp over Static). The informative MINFILE prior
favors Static prior across districts, but that win is partly an
artifact: MINFILE is a current snapshot and many post-2010 BCGS
discoveries are now MINFILE entries themselves, leaking the answer
into the prior.

**C.1 (noisy sensor).** The 3-by-3 $(\alpha, \beta)$ sweep covers
realistic reconnaissance values but not extreme cases (e.g., $\beta >
0.3$). The sensitivity to sensor quality is mild within the swept
range.

**C.2 (falsification).** Three hypotheses on a 20-by-20 toy subarea.
The falsification flag fires reliably; the categorical update
converges on the truth in 5 to 7 drills under matching-truth
scenarios. Production-scale falsification on real BCGT terrain is
the C.2 work integrated into the chapter rather than the
toy-only version that lived in the deeper notebook earlier.

**SARSOP and Tiger.** The Tiger-style problem was engineered to
expose the SARSOP advantage. Whether that structure (cost asymmetry
between exploration and commitment, claim cells with zero
information value) reflects real BCGT exploration is open; D.1.D
addresses it by replacing the synthetic priors with BCGS deposit-type
splits.

**D.1 (BCGT scale).** Two synthetic GP-correlated hypotheses
(non-overlapping NW and SE blobs) plus null. Per-episode truth
realization, one-shot discovery reward, top-K=20 candidate pruning.
All four policies tie within SEM on immediate reward. SARSOP wins on
belief-tracking accuracy (final posterior on truth: 0.82 vs greedy
0.67). The earlier draft of this chapter reported SARSOP winning by
57 points; that was an artifact of one canonical-realization and
aggregate-reward shortcuts now removed. The honest finding is that
the alpha-vector machinery scales (0.02 s solve per step at K=20),
but the planning advantage at scale depends on problem structure
that the synthetic priors do not produce.

The property-scale counterpart of this kind of decision-theoretic
drill recommendation, a Gaussian process drill-site recommender on
the 24 Bear Cub holes, sits in
[Chapter 1](https://johnsondevco.com/bearcub/bear_cub.html). The
Bear Cub recommender produces a workable next-hole pair even though
the spatial-only GP predicts pay-zone grade poorly in absolute terms,
because the posterior variance is a faithful map of where the data
is sparse even when the mean is unreliable.

## Next steps

D.1.D replaces the synthetic NW-vs-SE-blob hypotheses with real BCGS
deposit-type splits derived from the dh2loop labels covered in
Chapter 2. Real deposit-type priors will overlap heavily across
hypotheses (an alteration halo can sit inside two competing
geological model domains at once), which is a harder test than the
non-overlapping synthetic case. If real BCGT exploration has the
cost-asymmetry structure the Tiger experiment relies on (e.g.,
because drilling outside a claim type's expected geology wastes the
budget), the SARSOP advantage may transfer at scale; if not, the
honest finding will be that planning and greedy are equivalent on
real BCGT data. The deeper notebook carries the implementation
roadmap.

## Deeper notebooks

- [BCGT POMDP decision planning](./notebooks/bcgt/decision_planning.html): full implementation, GP kernel choice, particle-filter mechanics, the four-policy benchmark code, and the milestone roadmap details.

## Terminology

In order of first appearance in this chapter.

**POMDP** ("partially observable Markov decision process"). The math
framework for sequential decisions under uncertainty about the state
of the world, with noisy observations available at a cost. Drilling
is a textbook example.

**Prospectivity map / starting map / prior.** The per-cell probability
surface a planner starts with before any drilling. "Prior" is the
statistical term; "starting map" is the same thing in plain language.

**Gaussian process (GP).** A probability distribution over a
smoothly-varying surface, parameterized so neighboring cells covary
in a way calibrated to the geology. A GP-correlated prior says
"drilling cell A gives you partial information about cells B, C, D
within a calibrated correlation length."

**Discount factor ($\gamma$).** A number in $(0, 1)$ that down-weights
future rewards relative to immediate ones. We use $\gamma = 0.95$
throughout: a reward one step away is worth 95% of the same reward
right now.

**Bernoulli sensor.** A binary noisy sensor with two parameters:
$\alpha$ (false-positive rate) and $\beta$ (false-negative rate).
Returns 1 ("ore here") or 0 ("no ore here") on each drill, with
probabilities $1-\beta$ if there really is ore and $\alpha$ if there
isn't.

**Cox-Singer cutoff.** A USGS porphyry-Cu grade-tonnage benchmark
(0.2% Cu) that separates "this drill hit ore" from "this drill did
not." Used in the real-data retrospective.

**Belief.** A list of probabilities, one per hypothesis, that sum to
1. Says how confident the planner is in each geological story right
now. Starts at the prior and updates by Bayes' rule after every
observation.

**Uniform belief.** Belief where every hypothesis has equal
probability. The planner has no reason to favor any story.

**Hypothesis (in this chapter).** A complete geological story about
the area, with its own expected probability surface over cells.
**Single-hypothesis** carries one story; **multi-hypothesis** carries
several at once and tracks confidence in each one.

**Null hypothesis ($h_0$).** A special hypothesis in the multi-
hypothesis set representing "none of the geological stories on the
table fits the data." Added to the set so the planner can detect
when its whole hypothesis space is wrong.

**Random / GreedyMean / BayesianGreedy / POMCP / SARSOP.** Five
policies the chapter compares.

- **Random** picks any un-drilled cell uniformly at random.
- **GreedyMean** drills the highest-probability cell from the starting
  map. Never updates.
- **BayesianGreedy** drills the highest-probability cell under its
  current belief, which it updates after every drill. Thinks one
  step ahead.
- **POMCP** ("Partially Observable Monte Carlo Planning"). Same
  belief update as BayesianGreedy, plus simulated lookahead: before
  each drill it imagines many possible futures and picks the action
  whose simulated futures average best. Live planner (does work at
  decision time).
- **SARSOP** ("Successive Approximations of the Reachable Space under
  Optimal Policies"). Pre-computes a policy ahead of time and looks
  it up at decision time in microseconds. The policy is stored as a
  collection of alpha vectors.

**Alpha vector.** A list of numbers, one per hypothesis. Each number
says "if hypothesis $i$ were the truth, what would the value of
taking this action and then playing optimally afterward be." Each
alpha vector is tied to a particular action plan. SARSOP outputs
many of them.

**Dot product (of belief and alpha vector).** The expected reward of
taking the action associated with the alpha vector, under the
current belief. Computed as
$\sum_i \text{belief}[i] \times \alpha[i]$. The action whose alpha
vector gives the highest dot product is what the policy picks.

**Value function.** The function from beliefs to expected total
future reward under the optimal policy. SARSOP represents it as a
piecewise-linear surface (the maximum of all the alpha vector planes).

**Live planner / pre-computed planner.** "Live" planners do their
planning at decision time (POMCP). "Pre-computed" planners do their
planning once ahead of time and look up the answer fast at each step
(SARSOP). The POMDP literature uses "online" and "offline" for these,
but those words conflict with everyday computing usage.

**Tiger problem.** The textbook POMDP introduced by Kaelbling,
Littman, and Cassandra (1998): you are in a room with two doors; a
tiger is behind one and a reward is behind the other; you can listen
(noisy, cheap) to learn which is which, or you can commit by
opening a door (decisive, expensive if wrong). The optimal play is
usually to listen a few times to build confidence before committing.
The Tiger structure is the smallest POMDP that has a genuine
exploration-vs-exploitation tradeoff.

**Top-K candidate pruning.** A trick for running SARSOP on large
action spaces. At each step, only the K cells with the highest
expected deposit probability under the current belief are kept; the
rest are dropped for that step's solve. The subproblem is small
enough for SARSOP to solve fast; the candidate set is re-computed
at every step.

**One-shot discovery reward.** A reward semantic where the +50
discovery bonus is paid once per episode, on the first deposit cell
drilled. Subsequent drills only pay the drill cost. Matches the
real-exploration intuition that finding the deposit is the milestone,
not the count of deposit-region cells drilled.

## References

In order of first appearance.

- Mern, J., Yel, E., Caers, J., Kochenderfer, M. J. (2023).
  "Intelligent Prospector: implementing
  multi-step optimal sequential decision making for mineral
  exploration." *Geosci. Model Dev.* 16:289–307.
  [DOI 10.5194/gmd-16-289-2023](https://doi.org/10.5194/gmd-16-289-2023)
- Mern, J., Corso, A., Burch, T., House, M., Caers, J. (2024).
  "Intelligent Prospector v2.0: exploration drill
  planning under epistemic model uncertainty." arXiv 2410.10610.
  [arXiv:2410.10610](https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.10610)
- Silver, D., Veness, J. (2010). "Monte-Carlo planning in large POMDPs."
  *Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems* 23.
  [NeurIPS PDF](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2010/file/edfbe1afcf9246bb0d40eb4d8027d90f-Paper.pdf)
- Kurniawati, H., Hsu, D., Lee, W. S. (2008). "SARSOP: Efficient
  point-based POMDP planning by approximating optimally reachable
  belief spaces." *Robotics: Science and Systems IV.*
  [RSS PDF](https://www.roboticsproceedings.org/rss04/p9.pdf)
- Kaelbling, L. P., Littman, M. L., Cassandra, A. R. (1998).
  "Planning and acting in partially observable stochastic domains."
  *Artificial Intelligence* 101:99–134.
  [DOI 10.1016/S0004-3702(98)00023-X](https://doi.org/10.1016/S0004-3702(98)00023-X)
- Singer, D. A., Berger, V. I., Moring, B. C. (2008). "Porphyry copper
  deposits of the world: database and grade and tonnage models."
  USGS Open-File Report 2008-1155.
  [USGS report](https://pubs.usgs.gov/of/2008/1155/)

A consolidated repository-wide references page lives at
[references.html](references.html).

[← back to portfolio map](index.html)
